---
title: "Welcome"
---

# Aleo, Visualized

> An interactive guide to privacy on Aleo — for developers who want to
> **understand**, not memorize.

Most blockchain books are walls of definitions. This one is a sequence of
**questions** — the kind a smart engineer would actually ask when handed
their first Aleo project — paired with **interactive visualizations** that
let you see the answer rather than just read it.

If you can drag a slider, you can understand a Merkle proof.

---

## Who this is for

| You are... | This book gives you... |
|------------|------------------------|
| **A web2 / data engineer** new to web3 | Familiar mental models (UTXOs ≈ encrypted DB rows, Merkle roots ≈ content hashes) without crypto-bro vocabulary |
| **A Solidity / EVM developer** new to Aleo | Side-by-side comparisons (`record` vs ERC-20, `mapping` vs Solidity mapping, `transfer_*` family) |
| **A founder / PM scoping a privacy app** | Decision frameworks: what goes on-chain, what stays off-chain, what compliance looks like in a ZK world |
| **An auditor / security reviewer** | Anti-patterns that leak privacy, and the proof obligations the chain enforces |

You don't need to know zero-knowledge cryptography. You will after.

---

## How this book is structured

Every chapter follows the same shape:

1. **The question** — a concrete problem an engineer faces ("how do I prove a user isn't on a sanctions list without revealing the list?")
2. **The Aleo answer** — the primitive(s) Aleo offers, and why
3. **The visualization** — an interactive widget where you can poke at the system and watch it respond
4. **The implementation** — what the actual deployed code looks like, with citations to mainnet bytecode

When you finish a chapter, you should be able to **explain it to a colleague using only your own words and a whiteboard**. If you can't, that chapter failed and please open an issue.

---

## What's covered

::: {.callout-note}
This is an early-access book. New chapters land every two weeks. Subscribe to
the newsletter (link in footer) to get notified when each ships.
:::

### Foundations

- [Why ZK?](01-foundations/01-why-zk.qmd) — what privacy chains actually buy you
- [Records vs Mappings](01-foundations/02-records-vs-mappings.qmd) — Aleo's UTXO/account hybrid
- [Fields and curves](01-foundations/03-fields-and-curves.qmd) — what `field` actually is

### Cryptographic primitives

- [Hash functions](02-cryptographic-primitives/01-hashes.qmd) — BHP, Poseidon, why ZK-friendly matters
- [**Merkle trees** ✨](02-cryptographic-primitives/02-merkle-trees.qmd) — the killer chapter; non-inclusion proofs visualized
- [Commitments](02-cryptographic-primitives/03-commitments.qmd) — commit-reveal in pictures

### Privacy patterns

- [On-chain commitment + off-chain encrypted blob](03-privacy-patterns/01-on-chain-vs-off-chain.qmd) — the data-split decision
- [View-key disclosure topology](03-privacy-patterns/02-view-key-disclosure.qmd) — selective decryption
- [Burner wallets](03-privacy-patterns/03-burner-wallets.qmd) — the privacy shield UX pattern

### Real systems on mainnet

- [`credits.aleo`](04-real-systems/01-credits-aleo.qmd) — the native token, walked through line by line
- [`token_registry.aleo`](04-real-systems/02-token-registry.qmd) — multi-token standard with the live token table
- [USDCx and the freeze list](04-real-systems/03-usdcx-compliance.qmd) — compliance without surveillance
- [NullPay walkthrough](04-real-systems/04-nullpay-walkthrough.qmd) — a real privacy-payments dApp dissected

### Build it

- [Leo quickstart](05-build-it/01-leo-quickstart.qmd) — install, hello world, deploy
- [Your first program](05-build-it/02-first-program.qmd) — interactively edit Leo, watch it compile
- [Deploy walkthrough](05-build-it/03-deploy-walkthrough.qmd) — testnet → mainnet, the right way

---

## Why this book exists

Aleo is one of the most thoughtfully-designed privacy chains in production, but
its mental model is unlike anything in EVM-land. Records are consumed.
Mappings are public. Hashes are circuit-friendly. Compliance is a Merkle
tree on the chain and a database off it. None of this is hard once you've
seen it work — but until you do, it's just words.

This book is the visual companion I wish existed when I started building on
Aleo.

---

## How to read this

You can read straight through (recommended for first-timers) or jump to a
chapter — every chapter is self-contained. Each one has:

- **Inputs you can change** (sliders, text boxes, dropdowns)
- **Live diagrams** that update as you change inputs
- **Verified mainnet citations** — every claim about deployed Aleo programs
  is paired with a `curl`-able endpoint so you can check the bytecode yourself

If you want a paired AI-assistant for building on Aleo, check out
[`aleo-skills`](https://github.com/casualcomputer/aleo-skills) — a Claude Code
plugin and AI dev reference that pairs with this book.

---

## Status

🟢 **v0.1 — soft launch.** First two chapters live. Newsletter open.
The "✨" chapter ([Merkle trees](02-cryptographic-primitives/02-merkle-trees.qmd))
is the flagship — start there if you only have 15 minutes.
